edge: The technique designed for pose estimation is named Pose Integrated Gradient (PoseIG).
Pose Integrated Gradient (PoseIG) is presented as the first interpretability technique designed for pose estimation.
Pose Integrated Gradient (PoseIG) is designed specifically for pose estimation.
Pose Integrated Gradient (PoseIG) extends the concept of integrated gradients for pose estimation.
The concept of integrated gradients for pose estimation is extended to generate pixel-level attribution maps.
To enable comparison across different pose frameworks, different pose outputs are unified into a common output space.
A likelihood approximation function is used for gradient back-propagation in pose estimation.
To complement the qualitative insight from the attribution maps, three indices for quantitative analysis are proposed.
Different pose estimation frameworks are systematically compared to understand the impacts of network design.
Different pose estimation frameworks are systematically compared to understand the impacts of backbone.
Different pose estimation frameworks are systematically compared to understand the impacts of auxiliary tasks.
An under-explored inversion error is revealed for keypoints in body pose estimation.
Integrated Gradients (IG) is a commonly used attribution technique that can produce pixel-level attribution maps for various tasks, including pose estimation.
Pose Integrated Gradients (PoseIG) generates spatial attribution maps specifically designed for pose estimation.
Integrated Gradients (IG) and its derived variants can produce pixel-level attribution maps for various image and natural language classification tasks.
Based on the attribution maps generated by Pose Integrated Gradients, numerical indices can be proposed to characterize the attributions throughout the scene.
Based on the likelihood approximation function, we can back-propagate the gradients from predicted outputs to generate attribution maps for pose estimation.
Unlike classification models that output class likelihoods, pose estimation models can vary in output, ranging from spatial likelihoods to regressed coordinates.
Frameworks produce different outputs which need to be unified for meaningful comparison.
Different outputs are unified into a common output space for comparison.
Pixel-level attribution maps are used to generate visualizations for comparison across pose frameworks.
Visualizations are utilized for pose estimation in the context of attribution maps.
Indices are designed to analyze and diagnose pose estimation frameworks by characterizing the attributions.
FI measures the extent to which the foreground is considered in the attributions.

node_2: existing body and hand pose estimation frameworks
edge: PoseIG's attribution maps are used to study and analyze existing body and hand pose estimation frameworks.
Existing pose estimation frameworks are studied to provide insights on their design and architectures.
Existing models are diagnosed to identify overlooked issues in pose estimation, specifically related to MCP1 or knuckle joints.
MCP1 or knuckle joints exhibit artifically high performance in hand pose estimation, likely due to shortcut learning from data preprocessing.
Keypoint inversion is an under-explored phenomenon in pose estimation, where keypoints are predicted at the location of other keypoints.
Keypoint inversion errors in human pose estimation are diagnosed and simple solutions are proposed to alleviate these issues.
PoseIG is introduced as an interpretability technique for pose estimation, providing pixel-level attributions for comparing different works.
Three numerical indices are proposed to quantitatively characterize the attributions in the scene provided by PoseIG.

node_2: body and hand pose estimation frameworks
edge: Using PoseIG's attributions and indices, different body and hand pose estimation frameworks are analyzed and compared for design insights.
A shortcut problem is diagnosed in hand pose estimation and simple solutions are proposed to alleviate the issue.
Keypoint inversion errors are identified in human pose estimation, leading to proposed solutions for improvement.
New architectures and training strategies have been developed for pose estimation to enhance performance.
Pose estimation has seen improvements in accuracy through the development of new architectures.
Training strategies have contributed to the improvement in 2D and 3D keypoint prediction accuracy.

The terms 'joints' and 'ground truth joints' are related as they both refer to specific points in an image or data that are used in algorithms for network output prediction or pose estimation.
The terms 'Integrated Gradients' and 'input elements' are related as the former measures the contribution of the latter, i.e. image pixels, towards the prediction of the final network output.

The terms 'baseline' and 'image intensity' are related as the method described involves linear interpolation of the image intensity between them to determine the contribution of changes towards the network output prediction.
The terms 'function F' and 'pretrained deep network' are related as the former is a mapping function that represents the latter which processes the input image data for output prediction.
The terms 'input' and 'baseline' are related as the input image data is compared to a baseline image to determine the attribution map through integration along a specified path.

The terms 'Path Function' and 'original IG method' are related as they both discuss the methodology of determining the attribution map, with the former recommending the use of different baselines for pose estimation compared to the latter's recommendation of black images.
Attributions are biased towards pixels with dark colors, indicating a relationship between them.

The baseline image is progressively transformed into a Gaussian blur baseline, highlighting their interrelation.

PoseIG's attributions are verified for their faithfulness in the validation process.

PoseIG's attributions are verified for faithfulness using model randomization and image perturbation techniques. PoseIG is sensitive to network parameters as confirmed by changes in scene attributions with successive layers randomization.
PoseIG is sensitive to network parameters as evidenced by successively more changes in scene attributions with more layers randomized.

Image perturbation is used to confirm the faithfulness of PoseIG's attributions. Higher impact on pose accuracy is observed with perturbation of pixels having higher attributions compared to random perturbations.

Coordinate regression (RLE) is mentioned as having lower error per pixel (EPE) compared to explicit heatmaps. This suggests a relationship where RLE may be considered more effective in certain scenarios than explicit heatmaps.

Implicit heatmap (Integral Regression) and coordinate regression (RLE) are discussed as having lower EPE and utilizing more information from the rest of the body compared to explicit heatmap methods. This implies a relationship where implicit heatmaps and coordinate regression may share similarities in their approach.

HRNet is mentioned as leveraging features from different resolutions and capturing more spatial information compared to ResNet50. This indicates a relationship where HRNet is considered more versatile in feature extraction and spatial representation than ResNet50.

HRNet is compared to Simple Baseline ResNet50 in terms of global and local image evidence utilization for trunk and leaf joints. This relationship highlights the differences in spatial information preference between HRNet and Simple Baseline ResNet50.

LIR is defined as the proportion of low-interpretable samples for a given joint type. It helps identify joints with low interpretability, such as MCP or PIP. The LIR of MCP is 7.44%, while the LIR of MMCP reaches 16.25%.

A GCN is added as a refinement block after the output of the network to establish an explicit topology for poses, suggesting a sequential relationship between the two concepts.

Refinement with GCN decreases LIA(B) on the pairs with keypoint inversion predicted by the original baseline, indicating reduced dependence on spatial information.
